title: Late Stage Venture Is About Late Stage Founders | Andreessen Horowitz
gist: a16z's David George argues growth-stage venture has become the most important asset class, and that its returns are driven not by structure or valuations but by a rare type of 'late-stage founder' (Ali Ghodsi, the Collisons) who can keep compounding 100%+ growth indefinitely by making non-consensus capital-allocation decisions. The investor's edge is finding such founders, staying 'in the car' long-term, and resisting the instinct to impose a terminal value or replace them with professional CEOs.

insights.claim: a16z argues growth-stage venture has emerged as the most important asset class in the world, but that most people misunderstand it as a structural fundraising change or a story about valuations and staying private, when it is really about a specific type of person who can deploy dollars attractively indefinitely.
insights.speaker: David George
insights.so_what: Reframes how to underwrite late-stage deals: bet on founder quality/durability, not entry multiple or stage mechanics.
insights.quote: “Growth-stage venture” has emerged as its own asset class, and we think it’s the most important asset class in the world. But we also think that most people misunderstand what it’s about.

insights.claim: a16z contends that killer founders who understand how to apply increasingly powerful but still-undiffused technology will always be a more attractive destination for capital than other asset classes, and will create the majority of growth-stage venture's returns.
insights.speaker: David George
insights.so_what: Concentration thesis: a handful of elite founders drive aggregate returns, so manager selection and founder access matter more than diversification.
insights.quote: killer founders who understand how to use it will always be an attractive destination for investment dollars, versus anything else, and they will ultimately create the majority of the asset class’s returns.
insights.locator: The existence of founders like Ali Ghodsi

insights.claim: a16z asserts that elite founders such as Ali Ghodsi and the Collison brothers prove the right kind of person can keep growing a company ambitiously forever, implying no natural terminal point to their compounding.
insights.speaker: David George
insights.so_what: Supports holding/backing such founders far longer than conventional models assume, rather than rotating out at a fixed maturity.
insights.quote: The existence of founders like Ali Ghodsi or the Collisons has proven that the right kind of person can keep growing ambitiously, forever.
insights.locator: The existence of founders like Ali Ghodsi

themes.theme: Alpha is founder decision-making, not technology

insights.claim: a16z argues technology is only leverage and is not itself differentiating, because even optimally applied tech reaches its limits; the alpha of a founder-led company lies specifically in the founder's capital-allocation and decision-making, not in the technology stack.
insights.speaker: David George
insights.so_what: Tells investors to diligence founder judgment and decision quality, not just product/tech, as the durable source of returns.
insights.quote: The alpha of founder-led companies is in the founder’s decision-making.
insights.locator: The alpha in startups is the founder’s decision-making

insights.claim: a16z frames founder alpha as the ability to know when to follow tech best practices (most of the time) versus when to make brave non-consensus bets, citing Databricks's 'Lakehouse' architecture bet and Facebook's acquisition of Instagram as examples only a founder can legitimately make.
insights.speaker: David George
insights.so_what: Look for founders with a track record of well-timed contrarian bets and decisive M&A as a marker of durable edge.
insights.quote: whether it’s betting on a new architecture like Databricks’s “Lakehouse” model, or critical acquisitions like Facebook buying Instagram.
insights.locator: Founders carry the burden of deciding

insights.claim: a16z holds that the founder's opportunity set perpetually refreshes itself as technology improves, so a founder who can repeatedly spot non-obvious potential and apply it early generates alpha on every good decision.
insights.speaker: David George
insights.so_what: Improving tech continually resets the upside, justifying perpetual rather than time-boxed exposure to top founders.
insights.quote: The alpha is in every decision the founder makes that goes well, and the opportunity set perpetually refreshes itself as technology improves.
insights.locator: Venture Capital exists on the premise

themes.theme: Private markets as alpha vehicle vs other asset classes

insights.claim: a16z argues private markets have grown because of intense demand to access founder alpha, and frames the choice as either backing founders who immediately know what to do with new technology, or other asset classes with inflexible returns, lagging adaptability, and only principal to lose.
insights.speaker: David George
insights.so_what: Positions growth-stage private equity as structurally superior risk/reward to traditional asset classes for capital allocators seeking tech upside.
insights.quote: Any dollar invested into the software can either go to founders who immediately know what to do with unfolding technology potential... or they go into other asset classes where there is inflexibility in where the returns come from, lagging ability to adapt, and only your principal to lose.
insights.locator: Private markets have grown the way they have

themes.theme: Terminal-value error and the durability of elite founders

insights.claim: a16z argues investors make a persistent cognitive error by assuming high revenue growth must eventually slow to a reasonable terminal value, noting that not a single analyst believed Apple or Visa could sustain their growth rates for two decades, yet elite founders defy this.
insights.speaker: David George
insights.so_what: Warns that conventional terminal-value modeling systematically underprices the rare founder, creating a recurring mispricing to exploit.
insights.quote: in the public markets, not a single analyst thought that Apple or Visa could keep growing at the rates they did for two decades... It is human nature to eventually concede a terminal value that seems reasonable. But not for elite founders.
insights.locator: This cognitive error is shockingly persistent

insights.claim: a16z claims the old Silicon Valley practice of replacing the founder with a 'professional CEO' after Series B was foolish, and credits a16z (twenty years ago) with legitimizing a model where technical founders learned to become ruthless operators while keeping command of product and tech.
insights.speaker: David George
insights.so_what: Backs keeping founders in control as the value-creating default; founder removal is a red flag, not a fix.
insights.quote: It used to be common practice in Silicon Valley for Venture Capitalists to replace the founder with a “professional CEO” after Series B or so. With hindsight, we can see how foolish that practice was.
insights.locator: It’s hard to imagine how well things can go

insights.claim: a16z argues founders going public dislike not the pressure to perform but the pressure to do the consensus thing pervading analyst calls and shareholder feedback, which conflicts with the non-consensus bets that produce continued 100% growth.
insights.speaker: David George
insights.so_what: Explains the structural pull toward staying private and the appeal of long-duration private capital that protects founder autonomy.
insights.quote: What founders don’t like is the pressure to do the consensus thing, that pervades analyst calls and shareholder feedback, rather than make the bets that would result in that continued 100% growth.

insights.claim: a16z asserts each generation of founders learns from the previous one, and that this accumulated learning is why founders are staying private longer.
insights.speaker: David George
insights.so_what: Implies the trend toward delayed/avoided IPOs is durable and self-reinforcing, supporting continued growth of private capital pools.
insights.quote: And each generation of founders learns from the previous one; that’s why they’re staying private longer.

themes.theme: Trust, board dynamics, and 'in the car'

insights.claim: a16z argues the founder's job is a lonely existence with few people who can genuinely help or be trusted, citing Travis Kalanick's firing from Uber as proof that even a generational founder's investors may have other priorities.
insights.speaker: David George
insights.so_what: Founder-investor alignment and trust are scarce and material; misaligned boards can destroy a generational asset.
insights.quote: As they learned after Travis Kalanick got fired from Uber: you can be the greatest founder of a generation, but your investors may have other priorities.

insights.claim: a16z claims late-stage founders are protective of who they let 'in the car' because most early-stage investors and outside board members lack skin in the game and cannot help at the scale, pace, multi-product, and international expansion these founders require.
insights.speaker: David George
insights.so_what: Differentiation for growth investors is proving you can both genuinely help at scale AND be trusted; access depends on it.
insights.quote: Very few investors genuinely check both boxes: they can actually help, and they can actually be trusted.

insights.claim: a16z says it deliberately built its business to keep serving founders and remain 'in the car' for as long a time horizon as a founder could want, positioning investors as a tool kit for achieving the founder's objectives.
insights.speaker: David George
insights.so_what: Signals a16z's growth strategy of long-duration, service-heavy founder relationships as its competitive moat for deal access.
insights.quote: We built our business to make sure we can keep serving founders, and remain in the car, for as long a time horizon as the founder could ever want.

themes.theme: Increasing returns to scale via founders

insights.claim: a16z argues tech companies are attractive investments because technology yields increasing returns to scale, and that this holds true specifically because founders make it so by keeping up with the changing opportunity set and applying their leverage.
insights.speaker: David George
insights.so_what: Ties the increasing-returns case for tech equities directly to founder quality, so founder durability is the key variable to monitor.
insights.quote: The whole premise of tech companies as attractive investments is because technology yields increasing returns to scale. If there’s one thing we’ve learned... is that this is true because founders make it so.
